Benchmark.title: STIG Baseline for java Container Image
Benchmark.description:
This STIG baseline template applies DISA Security Technical Implementation Guide controls to the java container image (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy with OpenJDK 21 and wolfSSL FIPS).
Container Scope: - Application runtime security - Cryptographic enforcement - Audit logging - Access controls - Package management
Host Responsibilities (Out of Scope): - Kernel hardening - Boot process security - Physical security - Host network configuration - Host-level services

Profile.title: Container FIPS Baseline
Profile.description: Baseline profile for FIPS-compliant containerized applications on Ubuntu 22.04 (Jammy). Includes only controls applicable to container runtime environments.

Group.title: SRG-OS-000033-GPOS-00014
Group.description: The Ubuntu operating system must implement FIPS-validated cryptographic mechanisms.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must use FIPS-validated cryptographic mechanisms.
Rule.description:
Use of weak or untested encryption algorithms undermines the purposes of utilizing encryption to protect data. The operating system must implement cryptographic modules adhering to the higher standards approved by the federal government.
Container Implementation: - wolfSSL FIPS v5.2.3 (Certificate #4718) - OpenJDK 21 runtime with Java Security providers - wolfCrypt JNI (WolfCryptProvider) - JCE provider backed by wolfSSL FIPS - wolfSSL JNI (WolfSSLProvider) - JSSE provider for TLS/SSL

check.check-content:
Verify FIPS mode is enabled in the container:
1. Check Java Security providers: $ docker exec <container> grep "security.provider" /etc/java-21-openjdk/security/java.security
Expected output: security.provider.1=com.wolfssl.provider.jce.WolfCryptProvider security.provider.2=com.wolfssl.provider.jsse.WolfSSLProvider
2. Verify keystore type is FIPS-compliant: $ docker exec <container> grep "keystore.type" /etc/java-21-openjdk/security/java.security
Expected: keystore.type=WKS
3. Run FIPS validation test: $ docker run --rm java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips
Expected: Container starts successfully with FIPS provider initialization logged.
If FIPS providers are not configured or validation fails, this is a finding.

fix.fixtext:
Ensure container is built with FIPS-validated cryptographic modules:
1. Use wolfSSL FIPS v5.2.3 (Certificate #4718) 2. Build and install wolfCrypt JNI (JCE provider) 3. Build and install wolfSSL JNI (JSSE provider) 4. Configure java.security file: - security.provider.1=com.wolfssl.provider.jce.WolfCryptProvider - security.provider.2=com.wolfssl.provider.jsse.WolfSSLProvider - keystore.type=WKS 5. Set algorithm blocking policies (jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms, etc.) 6. Verify with FipsInitCheck.java and integrity-check.sh
Rebuild container if FIPS mode is not properly configured.

check.fixtext: Configure FIPS mode for the container

reference.dc:title: POC Validation Report
reference.dc:subject: Test Case 3: OS FIPS Status Check

Group.title: SRG-OS-000396-GPOS-00176
Group.description: The Ubuntu operating system must block non-FIPS approved cryptographic algorithms.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must block MD5 and SHA-1 cryptographic hash functions.
Rule.description:
Unapproved mechanisms that are used for authentication to the cryptographic module are not verified and therefore cannot be relied upon to provide confidentiality or integrity.
Container Implementation: - MD5, MD4, MD2: Blocked via jdk.certpath.disabledAlgorithms and jdk.jar.disabledAlgorithms - SHA-1: Blocked for TLS/JAR signing via jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms - DSA, RC4, DES, DESede: Blocked via jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms - Enforcement layer: java.security policy with wolfSSL FIPS backend

check.check-content:
Verify non-FIPS algorithms are blocked:
1. Check java.security policy for disabled algorithms: $ docker exec <container> grep "disabledAlgorithms" /etc/java-21-openjdk/security/java.security
Expected entries: jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms=SSLv3, TLS1.0, TLS1.1, RC4, DES, MD5withRSA, DSA, ... jdk.certpath.disabledAlgorithms=MD2, MD5, SHA1, ... jdk.jar.disabledAlgorithms=MD2, MD5, SHA1, ...
2. Run algorithm enforcement test: $ docker run --rm java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips
Expected: Test output showing blocked algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DSA, RC4, etc.)
If weak algorithms are not blocked, this is a finding.

fix.fixtext:
Configure runtime to block non-FIPS algorithms:
1. Edit /etc/java-21-openjdk/security/java.security 2. Set jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms to block weak ciphers (SSLv3, RC4, DES, etc.) 3. Set jdk.certpath.disabledAlgorithms to block weak hash functions (MD2, MD5, SHA1) 4. Set jdk.jar.disabledAlgorithms to block weak signatures 5. Configure wolfCrypt JNI and wolfSSL JNI as primary security providers 6. Verify enforcement with algorithm test suite
Test: docker run --rm java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips

reference.dc:title: POC Validation Report
reference.dc:subject: Test Case 1: Algorithm Enforcement via Java
Test Case 2: Java Cryptographic Validation

Group.title: SRG-OS-000057-GPOS-00027
Group.description: The Ubuntu operating system must generate audit records for privileged activities.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must log cryptographic operations and FIPS mode changes.
Rule.description:
Without generating audit records that are specific to the security and mission needs of the organization, it would be difficult to establish, correlate, and investigate the events relating to an incident or identify those responsible.
Container Implementation: - Audit log: STDOUT/container logs via docker-entrypoint.sh - Format: Structured logging with timestamps - Events: Container start, FIPS validation, integrity checks, provider initialization

check.check-content:
Verify audit logging is configured:
1. View container startup logs: $ docker logs <container>
Expected entries: - FIPS library integrity verification - wolfSSL FIPS provider initialization - Java Security provider configuration - FipsInitCheck validation results
2. Verify entrypoint script exists: $ docker exec <container> ls -la /docker-entrypoint.sh
If startup validation logs are missing or FIPS checks are not logged, this is a finding.

fix.fixtext:
Configure audit logging in entrypoint script:
1. Implement FIPS validation in docker-entrypoint.sh 2. Log integrity verification (integrity-check.sh) 3. Log Java Security provider initialization (FipsInitCheck.java) 4. Output all validation results to STDOUT for container logging 5. Include timestamps and clear status messages
Implementation: See docker-entrypoint.sh

Group.title: SRG-OS-000366-GPOS-00153
Group.description: The Ubuntu operating system must use cryptographic mechanisms to protect the integrity of audit information.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must verify package integrity using cryptographic signatures.
Rule.description:
Without cryptographic integrity protections, system packages could be replaced with malicious versions that contain security vulnerabilities or backdoors.
Container Implementation: - All packages from official Ubuntu Jammy repositories - APT signature verification enabled (default in Ubuntu) - SBOM provided for transparency

check.check-content:
Verify package integrity mechanisms:
1. Check APT configuration: $ docker exec <container> apt-config dump | grep "APT::Get::AllowUnauthenticated"
Expected: APT::Get::AllowUnauthenticated "false"; (or not set, default is false)
2. Verify SBOM exists: $ ls compliance/sbom-java-21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips.spdx.json
3. Check image signature: $ cosign verify --key cosign.pub java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips
If packages are not verified or SBOM is missing, this is a finding.

fix.fixtext:
Ensure package integrity verification:
1. Enable APT signature checking (default in Ubuntu Jammy) 2. Generate SBOM: ./compliance/generate-sbom.sh 3. Verify: cosign verify java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips

Group.title: SRG-OS-000480-GPOS-00227
Group.description: The Ubuntu operating system must enforce the principle of least privilege.

Rule.title: Ubuntu container must not run as root user.
Rule.description:
The principle of least privilege requires that processes operate with the minimum set of privileges necessary to perform their function.
Container Implementation: - Non-root user: appuser (UID 1001) - Minimal capabilities - Read-only root filesystem (where applicable)

check.check-content:
Verify container runs as non-root:
1. Check default user: $ docker inspect java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips | grep -i user
Expected: "User": "appuser" or "User": "1001"
2. Verify at runtime: $ docker run --rm java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips id
Expected: uid=1001(appuser) gid=1001(appuser)
If container runs as root (UID 0), this is a finding.

fix.fixtext:
Configure non-root user in Dockerfile:
RUN groupadd -g 1001 appuser && \ useradd -u 1001 -g appuser -s /bin/bash appuser
USER appuser
Rebuild image with non-root user configuration.

Group.title: SRG-OS-000480-GPOS-00228
Group.description: The Ubuntu operating system must set proper file and directory permissions.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must restrict file permissions to authorized users only.
Rule.description:
If files are accessible to unauthorized users, those users could potentially modify or delete sensitive information.
Container Implementation: - Application files: owned by appuser - Sensitive configs: 0600 permissions - Executables: 0755 permissions - No world-writable files

check.check-content:
Verify file permissions:
1. Check for world-writable files: $ docker run --rm java:21-jdk-jammy-ubuntu-22.04-fips \ find / -type f -perm -002 2>/dev/null
Expected: No output (no world-writable files)
2. Check sensitive file permissions: $ docker exec <container> ls -la /etc/java-21-openjdk/security/java.security
Expected: -rw-r--r-- (0644 or stricter)
If unauthorized permissions exist, this is a finding.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must restrict loading of kernel modules.
Rule.description:
CONTAINER EXCLUSION:
Reason: Containers do not have direct access to load kernel modules. Kernel module management is the responsibility of the host operating system.
Host Responsibility: The container host must implement kernel module restrictions according to DISA STIG requirements.
Container Impact: None (kernel shared with host)

fix.fixtext: N/A - Configure on container host according to DISA STIG requirements.

Rule.title: Ubuntu boot loader must be password protected.
Rule.description:
CONTAINER EXCLUSION:
Reason: Containers do not have a boot loader. Containers are started by the container runtime (Docker, containerd, etc.), not through a boot process.
Host Responsibility: The container host boot loader must be password protected according to DISA STIG requirements.
Container Impact: None (no boot process)

fix.fixtext: N/A - Configure on container host according to DISA STIG requirements.

Rule.title: Ubuntu must harden systemd services.
Rule.description:
CONTAINER EXCLUSION:
Reason: Minimal containers typically do not run systemd. This image uses a simple entrypoint script instead of systemd for service management.
Container Alternative: Process supervision is handled by the container runtime (Docker, Kubernetes) which provides: - Process monitoring - Automatic restart on failure - Health checks - Resource limits
Container Impact: Limited (systemd not present)

check.check-content:
Verify systemd is not running:
$ docker exec <container> ps aux | grep systemd
Expected: No systemd processes
Container uses entrypoint.sh for initialization instead.

fix.fixtext:
N/A - Container uses entrypoint script instead of systemd.
Process supervision provided by container runtime (Docker/Kubernetes).

note.title: Container Scope and Limitations
note.content:
This STIG template focuses on controls applicable to containerized applications. Many traditional OS-level controls are:
1. Host Responsibility: - Kernel hardening - Boot security - Physical security - Host network configuration
2. Not Applicable: - Boot loader configuration (containers don't boot) - Kernel module management (shared with host) - System services (minimal container design)
3. Container Runtime Responsibility: - Process isolation (namespaces, cgroups) - Resource limits - Network policies - Security profiles (AppArmor, SELinux)
For complete STIG compliance, this container must be deployed on a STIG-compliant host operating system.

note.title: FIPS Enforcement Strategy
note.content:
This container implements FIPS enforcement at multiple layers:
1. Library Level: wolfSSL FIPS v5.2.3 (Certificate #4718) 2. JCE Provider: wolfCrypt JNI (WolfCryptProvider) for cryptographic operations 3. JSSE Provider: wolfSSL JNI (WolfSSLProvider) for TLS/SSL 4. Runtime Level: OpenJDK 21 with Java Security provider configuration 5. Policy Level: java.security with disabled algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, RC4, DES, etc.)
This multi-layer approach provides defense-in-depth and ensures cryptographic operations cannot bypass FIPS validation.
